focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 7

Brine still sloshed through the gaps in Marrow Market's planks when Lale crossed behind Riva, and the scrubbing had raised splinters without touching the smell of opened mouths. Behind them, the hired crews rasped reed brooms over the starboard lane, working the mess Iven's silver had bought into something tradable again. Women on the lower walkways kept sloshing more through the gaps. Boys with reed brooms worried at strings of river weed caught around the posts. Someone had burned camphor in three cracked bowls and only managed to make the rot smell expensive. Lale kept her eyes on the planks. If she looked outward, noon would show her the places where corpses had wedged among melon crates and prayer boats because Iven had wanted one bright easy profit before Hymn Cut had settled.

Ink showed longer than blood on these boards. She knew that from work. Fat, blood, lampblack, indigo, rust, each stain took its own persuasion. What had happened here would not lift before the next rain. It might not lift before the monsoon broke. A season could keep a joke alive if enough people had watched it curdle.

Riva did not slow. She moved through shame the way she moved through rain: cursing it never got a boat unstuck. Daro walked with his mouth set in that handsome injured line he wore when the world failed to arrange itself around his significance. Nami kept looking over the railings at the water below the market, waiting for the black eddies to answer her if she stared hard enough. Iven hung a little to the rear, quieter than he had any right to be, his wet boots leaving half-moons on the planks.

Paska's house sat above the fish lane on a web of old pilings, the doorway curtained with stitched ray skin that clicked softly in the draft. Dried herbs hung under the...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...car on her chin pale under drying mud, the way she set her weight against the pole as if the world could still be argued into obedience through correct force.

"My hands are fine," he said.

"Good."

That was all. But he heard the strain in the word. It wasn't fear so much as offense, as if the river had introduced a kind of authority into the family economy that refused to be audited.

He turned and looked back once.

The shrine had already shrunk to bells and smoke and a patch of awning on the mud rise. Yet the room still pressed against him: Mera's jar at her chest, Fen staring over the broth pot, the brief hush before he answered for the cut. He could still hear Ilyan saying crowds don't kneel to a committee in that maddening low formal voice. He could still feel two string-callused fingers resting at his throat, finding the notch, testing the note, laying claim.

Tovin's warning from Reed Gate had not vanished. Shared burden. No single heir. He knew it, and he knew too that truth was not the same thing as what people reached for when they were frightened. The river amplified desire as readily as duty. Everyone had said that in one form or another. But what if desire was the only language people ever obeyed cleanly? What if Sereya had been wrong in her damage and right in her method? What if the route needed all five of them to work and only one of them to be believed?

He could already picture Riva poling, Lale tallying, Iven hustling ropes, Nami carrying lamps, and the prayers still coming to him. The arrangement should have made him spit. It opened in him instead like a flower fed on ditch water.

As the boat cleared the last bell post, the far bell rang once behind them, sharp and solitary.

Daro did not mistake it for proof.

He only let it mean what he wanted.
